files.SKILL.md:
# The Invariants

Good work is narrow, verified, and durable. Read the assignment, make the smallest correct change, prove it, and leave behind only the lesson that will help the next run.

## Read The Job

Read the current instruction end to end. Pull out every action verb: implement, fix, extend, report, document. Each verb is a deliverable.

Read the environment documentation and any persistent notes before editing. If tests or a validator exist, read them fully — they are executable requirements and they define the contract.

After enough reading, write. An imperfect implementation submitted early is worth more than a perfect plan that runs out of time.

## Establish Bearings

Before editing, know four things:

- where the target work lives,
- what behavior or artifact is expected,
- which nearby surfaces must remain unchanged,
- which check can prove the result.

For remote work, use the access method the environment provides. For multi-line changes, prefer a byte-stable transfer method over heredocs. For a single-line fix, a focused in-place edit is safer than replacing the whole file.

## Keep The Diff Honest

The task is the specification. Implement what it asks for, using the names, locations, formats, and conventions already present. Do not widen the change.

Never overwrite or revert existing working code. When the task asks to reuse a component with different parameters, extend or subclass it — do not rewrite it from scratch.

Preserve working contracts: public function names and signatures, existing file layout, data formats used by callers, audience boundaries in messages.

## Protect The Non-Negotiables

Write the simplest shape that satisfies every property at once. Do not fix properties one at a time — they compose badly in isolation.

Before committing, reject your own patch if any of these are true:

- A structure grows without a removal path. When entries expire and the container for a key becomes empty, the key itself must go — an empty container is still an entry. Growth without removal is a slow leak that no functional test catches.
- A read-decide-write sequence can be interleaved by concurrent callers. The critical section covers the read, the decision, and the write together — not just the write.
- Elapsed-time logic depends on a clock that can move backwards. Prefer a clock immune to wall-time adjustment.
- Behaviour at a boundary has not been walked — zero, one, maximum, off-by-one. Boundary errors are silent; they pass every test except the one that probes the edge.

Use this list while reasoning, not as a canned response. The current task decides which properties matter.

## Verify Honestly

Run whatever check the environment offers after every change. Read the output as written — do not infer, do not assume. A check you did not examine is not evidence.

A successful response is not proof of a changed resource. Re-read the target after every write. Command success is useful, but persisted state is the proof.

If verification fails, fix the cause and rerun. If the same test fails again after a prior fix attempt, the correction must be different in kind — not a surface variation of the same approach. Stop, re-read the failure, and understand why the prior approach was wrong before trying again.

When a fix works on one surface, apply it to related surfaces immediately. The pattern is already established — do not waste time rediscovering it.

## Keep Useful Memory

If a persistent note area exists, write compact notes that generalise:

- stable repo or environment facts,
- the verified test or command for this class of work,
- bug pattern, root cause, and fix,
- an invariant that prevented a mistake.

Do not store transcripts, fixture values, private content, or one-off names. Update stale notes instead of adding a second competing fact. Future runs need the pattern, not the story.

## Finish The Chain

When the task asks for a postmortem, be concrete and complete: name the bug class, cite the exact tests that caught it by their full node identifiers from the test results, state the root cause in one sentence, show the fix as a before-and-after code snippet from the relevant commit, and describe how to detect or avoid the class in future work.

When the task is documentation rather than code, produce the document directly from your prior notes — do not re-investigate the codebase. Read what you already recorded, synthesise it, and write the deliverable. Documentation is a first-class deliverable — produce it before the clock runs out.

End by comparing the original instruction to the actual state. Every requested outcome should be present and checked. Stage only the files the task named, describe the cause in the commit message, and commit before the session ends.
